A youth mobility scheme is not “free movement” in the EU’s terms and would not be “a betrayal of Brexit”. It would not really matter and would probably be a good thing. But that is not the point. Why this, now?…

posts.title: This trade debacle could go either way
posts.subtitle: We cannot afford to be bound to EU rules - we must have growth. Today, I made the case on GB News.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Previously, I have set out the appalling fiscal position we face due to soaring age-related spending without provision to cover it. We must return to growth or face ruin. That’s why it would be a huge mistake to bind ourselves to the EU’s anti-competitive regulations, regulations which the USA is trying to reform.In that context, this is a profound moment in the life of our country: it is reported this Labour government means to align to EU rules, which would rule out a trade agreement with the USA because the USA will not tolerate the EU’s practices. The Growth Commission has made clear what that would mean: -6.725% to -9.35% off GDP with EU alignment versus +7.9% with sensible reforms and a US free trade agreement. For reasons the Growth Commission has explained, this ought to be a no-brainer, one which helps us avoid ruin.How to Foster Growth by Advancing ACMD-Focused Trade Policy Reform in the US, published by the Growth Commission, explores the position of the USA.The paper contends that traditional trade tools such as tariffs, anti-dumping measures and countervailing duties cannot adequately address “behind-the-border” practices that distort markets. These Anti-Competitive Market Distortions (ACMDs) include not only high tariffs or explicit subsidies but also:Regulations that favour domestic firms, Weak property and intellectual rights protections and State-driven industrial favouritism. These distortions harm US firms’ competitiveness and undermine global economic growth.To tackle ACMDs, the Growth Commission suggests using an econometric model that evaluates three “pillars” in each country:International Competition (IC) – reflecting trade openness and the role ofexports,Domestic Competition (DC) – reflecting how pro-competitive a domesticregulatory system is,Property Rights Protection (PR) – covering all aspects of private property rightsincluding intellectual property.Countries receive scores indicating how far they deviate from optimal conditions. The United States could then impose a calibrated retaliatory tariff…

posts.title: Book Club: Bastiat
posts.subtitle: The Fighting for a Free Future Book Club loved Bastiat. We hope you do too.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: The Statue of Liberty reading a bookLast night, the Fighting for a Free Future Book Club discussed Frederic Bastiat's classics "What Is Seen and What Is Not Seen" and "The Candle Maker's Petition". We loved his timeless writing: we hope you do too. What Is Seen and What Is Not SeenThe Candle Maker's PetitionYou can find Bastiat’s collected works in full here. My speaking note introducing the session follows below.Speaking Note: Bastiat – What Is Seen and What Is Not Seen and The Candlemakers’ PetitionIntroduction to BastiatFrédéric Bastiat (1801–1850) was a French economist, writer, and politician—most famous for his wit, clarity and passionate defence of free trade and individual liberty.He was writing in the wake of the 1848 revolutions, a time of deep political and economic turmoil. His enduring gift to us is his ability to make profound economic insights vivid and accessible.1. What Is Seen and What Is Not SeenCore ideaBastiat introduces the concept of opportunity cost through the parable of the broken window. The fallacy he attacks: that destruction, or spending per se, stimulates the economy.“The seen”: the glazier earns money to repair the window. “The unseen”: what the shopkeeper could have done with that money instead—invest, save, or buy something new.Questions for discussionHow does this principle apply to modern policy—e.g. stimulus spending, public works, defence contracts?Are there current political narratives that rely on ignoring the “unseen”?Is the problem economic illiteracy, or a deeper moral and political instinct to do something, even at great cost?How does the fallacy of the broken window appear in post-crisis economic policies—e.g. after COVID-19 or in response to climate events?In politics, how often are policies judged only by their immediate effects rather than their long-term consequences?How does media coverage contribute to our focus on the “seen”? How can we counter this tendency in…

posts.title: Beware the bond markets
posts.subtitle: My book with Max Rangely set out how monetary policy has created an even larger bubble than that which burst in 2008. President Trump's actions threaten to pop it.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Governments borrow by issuing debt instruments called bonds. There’s a good primer here. The market in which bonds are traded is critically important: if bonds are being sold off, the effective interest rate – the yield – rises, causing immense financial pain to governments and households. It is for good reason that the bond market intimidates everybody.Beware the bond market Yesterday, Bloomberg reported Bond Markets Retreat as US Treasuries Lead Yield Jump Worldwide:A pullback from US Treasuries sent longer-term yields surging by the most since pandemic struck in 2020, deepening losses in what’s supposed to be a haven from financial turmoil and roiling markets abroad as investors sold government bonds to raise cash.This morning, the Telegraph reports, How Trump was forced to bow to reality of markets meltdown:Just as the UK’s former prime minister Liz Truss was forced to reverse her unfunded tax cuts after a bond vigilante rebellion in September 2022, Trump has suddenly about-turned on his “reciprocal” tariffs in the face of a looming global financial meltdown.The White House claims this was the plan all along: to announce tariffs to drive countries into negotiations. That may be true but if so, it was an immensely dangerous manoeuvre to so shock the bond markets.As if a trade war and “equity meltdown” were not enough, there is grave risk in the bond markets: governments and households are heavily indebted, sensitive to interest rates and in need of refinancing and yet there is a huge bubble in the bond markets, deliberately inflated by policymakers since the 2008 Global Financial Crisis and worsened during the pandemic as central banks bought bonds with new money.Former Bank of England Chief Economist Andy Haldane said in 2013:Let's be clear. We've intentionally blown the biggest government bond bubble in history. We need to be vigilant to…

posts.title: Tariffs are terrible – but Trump may save free trade
posts.subtitle: In politics, asking nicely for sound policy based on high principle does not work. Alas. Trump's policy is massively disruptive and potentially hugely damaging: how could good come of it?
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: President Trump’s tariff policy is causing widespread alarm and will doubtless do great damage to the world economy unless it is rolled back, which will not happen soon. What is going on and why would an American President overthrow free trade on a scale unprecedented in history?Free trade propaganda poster by ChatGPTThe argument for free tradeThe arguments for free trade are well established and unassailable.Free trade allows countries to specialise in producing goods and services in which they have a comparative advantage, leading to greater overall efficiency and prosperity. By reducing tariffs and non-tariff barriers such as regulations, resources are allocated according to market signals rather than political interests, encouraging innovation, lowering consumer prices and increasing choice. Competition from abroad disciplines domestic firms, preventing complacency and inefficiency, while access to global markets allows successful businesses to scale and grow.Trade also fosters interdependence, making conflict less likely, and spreads knowledge and technology across borders. Although it can disrupt particular industries, the overall gains from trade exceed the losses, raising national income. Attempts to protect domestic industries often backfire, leading to higher costs, retaliation and slower growth. In short, free trade expands the size of the economic pie, creates better jobs in the long run and strengthens peaceful cooperation between nations.However, unfortunately, countries operate anti-competitive market distortions and then, for example, dump subsidised products on markets for strategic reasons. Think subsidised Chinese steel for example. Countries also apply regulatory barriers as a form of anti-competitive practice, against the ideals of free trade and the interests of their people.Understanding Trump’s tariff policyTrade expert Shanker Singham recently explained President Trump’s tariff policy on Peter McCormack’s podcast. Here’s the clip:President Trump’s tariff policy can be understood in three parts:1. Strategic leverage: Trump has used tariffs as a negotiating tool to pressure foreign governments on issues…

posts.title: The Spring Statement
posts.subtitle: If reflecting on Labour's Spring Statement tomorrow fills you with dread, you are right. Thankfully, the Growth Commission has set out what a better government could do.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Yesterday, I attended the Spring Statement briefing from the Growth Commission. I left filled with hope: thanks to trade and competition expert Shanker Singham and Conservative peer Jon Moynihan, we have in our hands the recipe to return this country to economic growth and, with that growth, hope and then happiness.Parliament in a storm by Wonder GenAIAlas, it does not seem likely that tomorrow Rachel Reeves will announce the Government are adopting the Commission’s recommendations.I have written before on this substack and for Axiom Bitcoin about the fiscal situation we face. It is dire and worsening as a century of statism runs out of other people’s money and runs into the consequences of years of borrowing plus easy money.In the long run, a government will have to do difficult things to return to growth. Jon’s book sets out what must be done. In the short run, as the Growth Commission writes,The country is facing the triple whammy of a growth crisis, a fiscal crisis and an exports crisis and the government should take immediate action on a number of fronts if the UK is to escape continued economic stagnation, the Growth Commission reports today. Since last June, growth has fallen, together with GDP per capita, a good proxy for household income: people will feel worse off. Revenue shortfalls and spending overruns have led to fiscal crisis: compare bond yields to those after the Truss mini budget. Exports have fallen since the end of 2022 and, as Shanker set out in the meeting, it is hard to attribute the change to either Brexit or covid.Their prescription is bold:Recommendations on public spending and taxation,Regulatory reform including repealing the 2008 Climate Change Act, repealing the Town and Country Planning Act and abandoning several of Labour’s proposals, including the Employment Rights Bill, andAgreeing a…

posts.title: The "uniparty", benefits, public choice and political history
posts.subtitle: Talk of a "uniparty" aggravated me. Then I recalled I used to say, "Vote Labservative for same." What does history and public choice tell us about parties, their intentions and their actions in power?
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Is there a uniparty?After 14 years in Parliament – often opposing my own side and almost always opposed by Labour and the Liberal Democrats – being told I have been complicit in a “uniparty” has been deeply aggravating. Then I recalled I would say privately, “Vote Labservative for same,” in the years running up to my election...With Labour now echoing on benefits what the Conservatives were saying in 2010, and with the distance which comes from losing my seat, I am asking if there’s truth in the uniparty thesis. Are they all the same? What can the political history of my lifetime tell us? And what about public choice theory, the study of how politicians respond to incentives?I’ll canter through the political history of my lifetime, since 1971, then consider whether the incentives politicians face create the impression of a uniparty. A brief political history of the UK since 1970The Labour government today makes much the same complaints about the welfare system that the Conservatives made on coming to power in 2010, informed by the analysis of the Centre for Social Justice. Back then, the charge was clear: Labour had presided over a system that trapped people in dependency, discouraged work and embedded intergenerational poverty. Today, the Starmer government is saying much the same. The personnel change but it seems the problems, the complaints and the solutions do not. How true has that been in recent history?Britain in the 1970s: decline and discordIn the 1970s, Britain was in the grip of economic stagnation, industrial strife and political instability. The post-war consensus—built on high taxation, nationalised industry and a welfare state designed for a more disciplined and deferential society—was crumbling. Inflation soared, union power paralysed industry, and the country became reliant on IMF bailouts. Government switched hands frequently—Heath’s Conservatives struggled with…

posts.title: We Should All Pay Attention to What is Happening in Argentina
posts.subtitle: President Javier Milei is implementing a programme of radical free market reform that it is already bearing fruit for ordinary Argentinians and his popularity. UK politicians must take note.
posts.author: Harry Richer
posts.text: On 1st March, President Javier Milei delivered his State of the Nation address to the Argentinian Congress.Since he was elected in November 2023, the world’s only openly libertarian president has implemented a programme of radical free market reform. Unlike many who take office, Milei has talked the talk and turned his rhetoric into action. That action is now bearing extraordinary fruit for ordinary Argentinians.What is going on in Argentina is something the modern world has never before seen. A libertarian President, elected on a radically free market agenda, who is actually implementing these ideas and has been working to shrink the size of the state from day one. I am reluctant to call it an “experiment”1, as some have: Milei’s policies are rooted in an expansive tradition of free market ideas, of which too many are unacquainted. Few members of our Parliament could even quote figures like Hayek or Mises, let alone explain their work on national television. @Milei_Explains - https://x.com/Milei_Explains/status/1739486111901098427 I recall conversations with other liberty-minded parliamentary staffers, and some liberty-minded parliamentarians, when Milei was elected. We discussed if he would even be able to implement his agenda in the face of the legacy corporatist Argentinian opposition and, indeed, if he were to implement this vision and it then failed, would we be forced to question our own beliefs.Thankfully, the early stages of Milei’s agenda have been a roaring success, as some have noticed. The Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) is the USA’s comparatively less radical attempt to replicate these successes and shrink the size of America’s bloated state.The UK must now take note. Our problems have been caused by a crisis of big government, not because we have been taxing too little, spending too frugally, or not regulating enough. The UK will only begin to prosper again when…

posts.title: The Intellectuals and Socialism plus Isaiah’s Job
posts.subtitle: Moving upstream, winning in the long term: introducing the Fighting for a Free Future Book Club. This is the first of a series of my speaking notes as Chairman.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Losing my seat in the Commons has liberated much more time for politics and where this country goes matters: that’s why I have launched the Fighting for a Free Future project, comprising this Substack, a community amplifying the good think tanks and a book club to equip and encourage the next generation.Time to move upstream in the fight for freedom.Last night we held our third discussion. My speaking note as Chairman follows below. We have previously studied, amongst other things, How to Advance Liberty and the Principles for a Free Society. I intend to publish my introductory speaking note the morning after every book club. I hope these are useful. Please get in touch if you would like to be considered to join us for beer, pizza and books.Book club speaking notes Nock and Hayek 10 MarchSpeaking Notes: Hayek’s The Intellectuals and Socialismhttps://cdn.mises.org/Intellectuals%20and%20Socialism_4.pdfOverview• Hayek argues that socialism prevails not because of direct economic reasoning but because of the influence of intellectuals—those who disseminate and interpret ideas rather than generate them.• These intellectuals act as intermediaries between scholars and the public, shaping the climate of opinion over time.• The market is losing the battle of ideas because classical liberals fail to engage the intellectual class effectively.Key Themes for Discussion1. The Role of Intellectuals• Intellectuals do not create original ideas but filter, simplify, and popularise them.• Socialism’s appeal is due to its simplicity and moral rhetoric rather than its economic validity.• Intellectuals tend to favour collectivist solutions because they align with their desire for rational planning and control.2. Why Free Market Ideas Struggle• Classical liberals often focus on technical economic arguments rather than moral and philosophical narratives.• Socialism offers a utopian vision, whereas free market advocacy tends to be reactive and defensive.• The intelligentsia is drawn to big-picture, abstract solutions rather than…

posts.title: Right turn ahead for Reeves and Starmer
posts.subtitle: Against all their instincts, Labour are about to turn further right. Centre-right politicians and think tanks must drive them forward in a situation which is more serious than commonly understood.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: The BBC reports, Chancellor set to cut welfare spending by billions. But what was Rachel Reeves saying in the 2010 parliament, when George Osborne was trying to balance the books after the Global Financial Crisis and Reeves and I had not long been elected?Right turn ahead for Reeves and Starmer – image by DALL•EHere is a sample of her stridency:One week before Christmas we have a chance to bring hope and relief to hundreds of thousands of people who are struggling to stay in their home, pay the bills and put food on the table by scrapping this cruel and punitive tax on bedrooms, which is yet another example of Tory welfare waste.As far as I can tell, Labour have not yet abolished “the bedroom tax” which Reeves so frequently described as “cruel”, nor have they reversed many of our other reforms to welfare. They can’t afford to, for reasons I will come to.Now they wish to go further. Justice Secretary Shabana Mahmood told the BBC:"There's a moral case here for making sure that people who can work are able to work and there's a practical point here as well, because our current situation is unsustainable.”Alas that they did not work this out 15 years ago: we might have made more progress. But that is politics for you1. What is the situation now, what will happen next and how should the centre-right family respond in fighting for a free future?The situation todayThe fiscal situation today is broadly as follows:Vast, increasingly unaffordable state spending,At or beyond the practical limits of taxation,With immense debt accumulated and Years of cheap credit and quantitative easing (colloquially, “money printing”)I have written about this before:The system of society which is now failing is not one based on the principles of a free society, with limited government,…

posts.title: Trump vs Zelenskyy
posts.subtitle: If you don't want disaster, have short press conferences. But President Trump wanted it to run on – why? Power politics: politicians feel the heat – they don't see the light.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: I love high-minded idealism as much as anyone, but don’t expect it from the USA for a few years. No amount of fulminating about how President Trump treats President Zelenskyy appallingly by whichever historical comparison will make him behave any differently. You may just have to be disappointed.The complete press conference with Presidents Trump and Zelenskyy may be found here. It lasted nearly 50 minutes, encompassed heated exchanges and appeared to be a disaster for Ukraine and for European security. It was painful to watch.What factors produced this event and why? I wish I could say I had never seen anything like it but, alas, I certainly have, though of course at lower stakes.Bears in the Oval Office, DALL.EIn the midst of major disagreements, keep press conferences short and formalIn ordinary circumstances where you wish to avoid unpleasantness over major disagreements among friends, keep press conferences short and formal.As a Northern Ireland minister, I often attended UK-Ireland press conferences. Relations were cordial, and while disagreements existed, the presentation of them was managed carefully by both sides. We did not want an escalation; we wanted a way through. Ministers and officials rightly saw press conferences as risky, so they were brief, formal and well-prepared. We spoke to the press, overwhelmingly not to each other, avoiding confrontation.The Trump-Zelenskyy press conference was far looser. The seating encouraged debate amongst politicians, not just Q&A with journalists. Given Trump’s likely statements and Zelenskyy’s inevitable reactions, the layout was a disaster waiting to happen. Had Trump wanted to avoid a public row, he could have ended it after 20-30 minutes. Instead, as he said, he prolonged it, ensuring disagreements surfaced publicly.This was a trap into which Zelenskyy walked.President Zelenskyy’s mishandlingChoices have consequences which we may or may not foresee. I understand why President Zelenskyy wears combat…

posts.title: Love of country, for good and ill
posts.subtitle: We can have too much of a good thing: nationalism becomes militant and dangerous. Legitimate love for our country is rightly tempered by respect for others.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Sometimes it seems we live in a world where every virtue is twisted and perverted into vice. Love for country is one such virtue, for it is one element which provides for a healthy identity and a basis for those moral sentiments which bind us together as a voluntary community against adversity, both everyday and existential.Display RAF Red Arrows Hawk on Downing Street, a proud symbol of the UK.A modest, gentle and usually automatic love for our own country has been for too long condemned as wickedness and held in contempt: that is itself a wicked vice which has done no end of harm, not least in fomenting an equal and opposite reaction. I feel little need to be generous to those “progressives” who so stripped many of pride in our history, mixed as it inevitably is. But perhaps they were motivated by the other notorious perversion of gentle and unassuming patriotism: a militant nationalism which seeks to condemn and dominate others.We should make no mistake: in the first half of the twentieth century, the rise of totalitarianism, particularly Nazism, was a consequence of economic interventionism combined with militant nationalism.That is why so many are so shocked that the former East Germany has handed second place and 151 seats in the Bundestag to far-right1 Alternative for Germany (AfD). The crimes of Germany during the Second World War still drive a deep rift into that country’s history and national psyche. That Germans should have voted for a party of nationalism is a fearful thing for anyone who has studied the causes of that tyranny.A grand coalition between conservatives and social democrats now seems probable to keep AfD out of power. That will inevitably create correspondingly grand problems as ideological opponents struggle to govern together to deny the politics of their worst…

posts.title: Bitcoin, gold and the Bank of England
posts.subtitle: The most important subject we face is discussed too little: how money is created and how the process corrupts society. My speech on "Money Creation and Society".
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: The methods of money production in society today are profoundly corrupting in ways that would matter to everyone if they were clearly understood. Last week, I appeared again on Peter McCormack’s podcast – out soon – with CoinDesk’s James Van Straten for an intergenerational perspective on political economy and Bitcoin. In the podcast, they showed clips from my 2014 debate in the House of Commons on Money Creation and Society. That debate saw the first mention of Bitcoin in the Commons and it was the first on the monetary system since the 1844 Bank Charter Act. It turns out it has meant more, to more people, than I knew or could have hoped at the time. I am best known for my campaigns to restore democratic control of political power by leaving the EU, to oppose excessive Covid lockdowns and restrictions and to overthrow untenable Net Zero energy policies. Those policies are of the first importance and I am proud of my work, but towering above them is the slow collapse of our present monetary system, with all the corruption of our society it entails.Today, as gold flows out of the Bank of England, I am prompted to post that speech abridged as Coindesk reports U.S. Crypto Task Force to Focus on Delivering National Bitcoin Reserve.The flaws in our system of money and their devastating consequences were among the reasons I gave up being a business software engineer for parliament1. It remains my opinion that the state is not likely to rescue us from a collapse of that monetary orthodoxy, and that privately-issued monies like Bitcoin and gold are the only viable route to a monetary reconstruction. The post-war social democratic model of western civilisation will continue its unravelling as politicians and society seek to live beyond taxpayers’ means and…

posts.title: Too soon Nigel. Too soon.
posts.subtitle: The future of our nation already rests on the shoulders of Nigel Farage. He has trapped himself by his success and seems to know it. He will decide the next government.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Sources close to the Reform UK leader told The Mail on Sunday that he would be open to working with the former PM ‘in the national interest’ to defeat Labour.Too soon Nigel. Too soon.Following his October 2024 article in the Telegraph, I set out The Problem with Nigel Farage: he alienates too many people who might vote for a centre-right party and, though much of his critique of the Conservatives is right, frustration is not a strategy. I concluded that the impediments to moving from 5 MPs to forming a capable government make Reform a mistake: it would take decades to succeed.Since then, things have moved on.Assuming Reform and the Conservatives appeal mostly to the same voters, there are only three ways for the centre-right to form a government: Reform wipes out the ConservativesThe Conservatives wipe out ReformSome kind of deal is done to bring the right together, whether a merger or an electoral pact leading to a coalition.The politics of all three are very difficult and unsatisfactory. The future seems wearily inevitable, even as Reform take votes from Labour and mobilise those who do not usually vote. As this weekend’s press salivates over the prospects of a deal, here is what I think will happen, bearing in mind all I know about the characters involved, their motivations and their weaknesses.To be clear, I am not much interested in party, nor have I ever been. My goal is a future for the UK which is happy, prosperous and free. That requires a competent and capable centre-right government of any party, or a coalition, willing to make the hard choices over many years to avoid disaster and set us on the right path. Press coverageOver the course of today, an article in The Mail on Sunday has evolved from “sources close…

posts.title: Return to Growth
posts.subtitle: There is hope. Lord Jon Moynihan's "Return to Growth" sets out a clear plan. Of 15 years. The sooner we start...
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Over the past week, I was glad to appear on Sunday Morning with Trevor Phillips and again on GB News. The relevant clips are above. I talked about the spending challenge we face and promoted Lord Jon Moynihan’s excellent new book, Return to Growth (vol 1, vol 2), which offers a clear plan to a more hopeful future1. My full review of Jon’s book will be in the next edition of The House Magazine. Return to Growth - How to Fix the Economy, Lord Jon MoynihanIn the meantime, I would welcome your feedback on my attempts to communicate on TV these big issues which are fundamental to fighting for a free future. The challenge is to make a compelling argument about complicated material in such a way as not to cut too hard against people’s expectations… Would returning to the EU solve our growth problem?With the post-war social democratic system in collapse as it runs out of young workers to meet pension, health and care obligations, would returning to the EU solve our growth problem? You only need to look at GDP data to see that EU membership has long meant stagnation.Selected GDP data from Our World in Data: EU membership has long meant stagnation.To listen to the propaganda which we have endured for 9 long years, one would think the EU were growing at a fair pace and that we had cut ourselves off. Neither is true.EU GDP has been stagnant for decades. Our leaving made little difference to our GDP. And our goods trade is not greatly affected: from the Commons Library brief, you can see the pattern yourself - don’t forget covid or the inevitable discontinuity at the end of the transition period:The idea that returning to the EU would generate sufficient growth to deal with our…

posts.title: Compromise – a dirty word
posts.subtitle: We all prefer total and final victory. It is rare. Leaders compromise. And that is tough.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Today is the fifth anniversary of our exit from the European Union. Throughout the process, I was one of the leading figures on the leave side, in various capacities1. I was often asked, “Why won’t you compromise?!”The UK has had a tough time. I am sticking with her.The answer of course was that the consequences of the compromises at hand would have been intolerable. For example, the proposed 2018 deal over which I resigned as a Brexit minister with David Davis would have firmly split the Conservative Party as we collapsed into a general election against a Corbyn-led Labour Party: they would have won and that would have been a long-term catastrophe for the UK2. Ultimately, I did compromise, backing Rishi Sunak’s Windsor Framework for Northern Ireland. It was a hard compromise indeed and it has proven a deeply painful experience over which I have lost valuable friends and allies. The circumstances illustrate wider lessons about compromise and the price of it. I will set out the circumstances as briefly as feasible and then explore some general lessons about politics and compromise for those who aspire to leadership.Borders, the European Union and Northern IrelandThanks to governments doing things, borders are a real nuisance. One way to abolish borders is to be in the same country, or what amounts to it: the EU. Countries which are in the EU’s Schengen Zone have no checks on people moving between them. The UK and Ireland are not in Schengen: we have our own Common Travel Area apart from the EU. Leaving the EU never risked it.The European Free Trade Area (EFTA) countries - Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway and Switzerland - are in Schengen but not the EU or the EU Customs Union. Switzerland isn’t in the European Economic Area (EEA) but gains access by…

posts.title: Debating the death penalty
posts.subtitle: Many criminals do not deserve to live but the death penalty cannot be undone and mistakes can be made. There are further good reasons why it is abolished.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Today, I accepted an invitation from GB News to debate the death penalty.I am very clear that Rudakubana does not deserve to keep his life but there are good reasons why we do not have the death penalty in the UK.The moral weight of crime and punishmentThere is no denying that some crimes are so heinous, so devastating, that they test our capacity for mercy. Individuals who commit such acts do not deserve to live. This instinct arises from our deep sense of justice and the desire to see a proportional response to unimaginable wrongs. It is also a reflection of the need to uphold societal norms and deter the most grievous offences.The irreversibility of the death penalty However, the death penalty carries a weight of its own: its finality. If a mistake is made in sentencing, there is no way to undo it. Alas, history is replete with examples of individuals who were later exonerated through new evidence, confessions, or advancements in forensic science. A wrongful execution is not merely a legal failing; it is a moral catastrophe that undermines the very justice the system seeks to deliver.The risk of fallibility in justice systemsNo system of justice is perfect. Biases, errors in investigation, or overzealous prosecution can lead to miscarriages of justice. While some argue that the risk of error is minimal, even the smallest possibility of executing an innocent person must give us pause. The death penalty, once enacted, leaves no room for redemption, restitution, or reconsideration.The role of a just societyA society's justice system should reflect its values. By rejecting the death penalty, a society signals its commitment to human dignity, even in the face of inhuman acts, thereby rejecting the basis of the mass crimes of the past committed by tyrannical states.This does not mean condoning…

posts.title: Journalists: five scenarios
posts.subtitle: You are making the news. Here’s how to survive and win.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: The dread day has come. you cannot apply Rule 1. What is to be done? These are the five key situations you will face as an MP: the press release, the proactive individual briefing, the unsolicited inquiry, the scrum and lunch.A scrum. These things happen. Especially when you make it so.Each scenario is rated for opportunity, risk and difficulty, with key tips not just to survive to fight, but to win in each engagement. Enjoy!Subscribe nowFive scenariosThe press releaseOpportunity: low; risk: low; difficulty: low.If you can’t manage a local press release, you shouldn’t be in politics. Communicating a visit to a hospital, praising staff and promising public support is straightforward. It’s low-risk and usually newsworthy unless your local paper is hostile.National press releases are different: most end up in the bin. Remember rule 2 (their interests) and rule 5 (motivations). Sending a national release about local events or your heroic party loyalty is good only for a laugh. Don’t be a plonker.Use national releases for genuinely significant events: maybe you’re challenging government policy with real parliamentary backing, championing a major bill, running a leadership campaign, or needing to address a scandal. If it’s not national news, don’t bother. If it is, follow rule 9: be accurate, brief, and clear. Put the big news up top. Are you exposing a scandal? Highlighting a threat to the public good? Threatening a party split? Offering a new solution? Make it obvious quickly.Write with your stakeholders in mind, anticipating how critics will twist your words. If it is an event, double-check logistics. Above all, keep it concise.The proactive individual briefingOpportunity: medium-high; risk: low; difficulty: medium.So now you are up to something and you are sufficiently well known that you can call or text a journalist and they will pick up or agree to see…

posts.title: Journalists – ten rules for working with the Press.
posts.subtitle: Can't live with them. Can't deport them to South Sudan. Ten rules for working with the Press.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Some limited humour follows. God knows they deserve it - you know who you are. In my next paid post, I’ll apply the rules in five scenarios.Journalists. They will get you if they can. It is in their nature.Ten rules for working with journalistsCommunicating effectively with the press is a vital skill for anyone seeking to advance ideas, represent a community or defend a record. Handled well, media engagement can steer events, shape narratives and protect reputations. Mishandled, it can open the door to needless controversy, ruined reputations and endless work.On my election in 2010, I had no idea about journalists: they were all terrifying, local and national. By the time the EU referendum result was in, I had been well exposed to print and TV journalism. My exposure then dramatically accelerated as the Brexit controversy grew, leaving me well equipped to get through my work on Covid and Net Zero. Today, I take snippy diary coverage as merely a helpful advert: thanks The Times!The first rule of briefing journalists: don’t brief journalistsMy parliamentary staff induction contained a simple rule:Do not brief journalists or the media without permission.Parliamentary staff tend to arrive from university and tend to arrive somewhat idealistic. It was necessary to explain what a fishing expedition may look like. “Steve said I should ask you for the list of rebel MPs,” on the phone for example.I know readers will find this hard to believe, but journalists will sometimes use subtlety and subterfuge to stand up a story. There was one safe principle: do not give out information and never brief a journalist: refer them to me. Even if you have done nothing wrong, or your side of the controversy is obviously on the moral high ground, speaking to journalists can too easily go terribly badly wrong. It…

posts.title: Money, money, money!
posts.subtitle: Getting realistic about money: there’s not much in politics - and that makes you more powerful. You could donate effectively to the party of your choice at an affordable level.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: There is a vast amount of absolute nonsense talked about money in politics, particularly in relation to how much of it is about, how it comes to be donated and how much gets “trousered” by MPs. Money, money, money! There’s not much in politics - and that makes you more powerful.This article explodes some myths and covers the realities of political funding in the UK, why you should consider donating and how to do so effectively. It is about the perspective of the individual candidate for elected office and why your relatively affordable donation counts.1This is not advice. If you are a candidate or agent, you must familiarise yourself with the rules.Exploding myths - the reality of UK political fundingThe sums of money spent in UK political campaigning are relatively small and highly regulated. The regulator is The Electoral Commission (EC). Some donations go to the national party and some to local constituency associations.Political finance is highly transparent. If you want to know which political parties are registered with the EC, where their money comes from and how they are spending it, visit the political finance database. If you want to know what money an individual MP has received and what financial interests they have, it is declared here. There are tighter requirements on ministers, whose interests are separately published. None of this is secret. Contrary to endless stories, MPs are not “trousering” money: they are struggling to raise relatively small sums to cover the basics of political campaigning.The EC guidance on UK Parliamentary general election spending may be found here. There is a regulated period of about a year.The spending limits on parties are set out here. The amount of money a party could spend in the regulated period for the 2024 general election was £54,010 per seat contested.…

posts.title: Zero tolerance: the politics of child sexual exploitation by gangs
posts.subtitle: Politicians, officials and the police must show an absolute commitment to the rule of law, including equal application, plus the courage staunchly to defend victims and the innocent.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Some issues in public life should be very easy: safeguarding children as an absolute priority is one of them. Avoiding heaping shame on the innocent is another. The longstanding principles of justice in our country ought to make both ideas apparent.And yet, as the Telegraph sets out, in some parts of our country those charged with sacred duties failed in them, scandalously and over many years. It is an utter disgrace and people are right to be angry. Plainly, steps must now be taken at the national level to ensure this never happens again. There should be a national public inquiry: investigations cannot now be left to local authorities. Through it all, even in that righteous anger, there will be no justice in condemning innocent people by association because of their identity.Posts tagged “Op Ribbon” at stevebaker.infoGiven demands for a public inquiry into systematic child sexual exploitation to which a blind eye was turned over quite wrong and cowardly sensitivities about race relations, I revisited what happened in Wycombe in 2012 when a group of Asian men were arrested, charged and tried for similar alleged offences. Over one fifth of the population in Wycombe is Asian, overwhelmingly hailing from Pakistan.The outcome of the case may be found here and my response to events at the time may be found here: seven men were found not guilty and the jury failed to reach a verdict on three men. I am satisfied that in Wycombe and Thames Valley, the law was applied equally to all without fear or favour. The pursuit of justice for and the protection of victims was paramount in everyone’s minds. Appropriate steps were taken to manage tensions in our community. The rest of this article sets out what ought and ought not to be done in the handling…

posts.title: Happy new year!
posts.subtitle: Wouldn't we all be better off if we made more of an effort to listen to those that we disagree with?
posts.author: Steve Baker, Prof Paul Dolan
posts.text: To promote a free society, we have a considerable battle to be heard by people who disagree with us, and we have much to learn from them too.That’s why I launched The Provocation People with my friend and LSE behavioural scientist, Professor Paul Dolan. Our society and the organisations within it need to embed tolerance by design in order to make better decisions and be more innovative, productive and happier. We need diversity of thought and The Provocation People is here to help.To start the year, Paul released our episode of his Breaking Beliefism podcast and we recorded the above discussion of Beliefism, New Year’s resolutions and happiness by design. I am incredibly proud of the range and quality of our discussion in the full Breaking Beliefism podcast. We covered:Beliefism, Paul’s term for discriminating against people who hold different beliefs.The difficulties and dangers of groupthink, particularly during the COVID-19 pandemic.The role of fear in entrenching majority views and alienating those who question them.The interplay between beliefs, identity, and social ties.Challenges in discussing complex policy without being branded an extremist.Adversarial collaboration: cooperating on specific issues while disagreeing on others.How open disagreement is more explicit in politics, sometimes making it (paradoxically) more straightforward to collaborate across party lines.The notion that people across the political spectrum do care about fairness, but differ on definitions and policy solutions.Plus Christianity, self-importance, snobbery, skydiving and Coldplay.If we are going to fight for a free future successfully in 2025, we will need to be better at engaging with those we disagree with. That is what The Provocation People is about. A very happy new year and if you like the podcast, please share it. There’s plenty of social media on my channels.

posts.title: Thank you and Merry Christmas!
posts.subtitle: To all my subscribers, many thanks and Godspeed in 2025
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Merry Christmas!A huge thank you to everyone subscribing to Fighting for a Free Future, paid and free. You are all very much appreciated.This was an extraordinary year: having you here to share reflections on the madness that is political life may have helped me recover from it. The top five most viewed posts were:The Principles for a Free SocietyThe Conservative party is the best hope we have for good governmentPower corruptsThe problem with Nigel FarageFree speech, justice and the perils of state overreachNext year, I plan to complete a series on practical politics with an article on how politics is funded and how to get the most out of donating, then one on how people can work effectively with their MP.I am keen to have your views: what would you like me to write about? Current events from a practical libertarian standpoint? Economics and the challenges we face? Geopolitics? Government reform? Leadership? Shall I do two series inspired by Bismarck: one on how to win friends and influence people, the other on how to fight with political pirates? My constant theme is bound to be the need to cut spending, cut taxes, regulate better and live within our means so that an economy founded on sound money can generate the just, inclusive and sustainable prosperity we need.Please let me know in the comments. In the meantime, a very Merry Christmas and for paid subscribers only, an announcement below the paywall.Starting in January, for an initial three months and thanks to generous supporters, I have hired a brilliant Director of the Fighting for a Free Future Project: let’s see if we can step things up a few gears. Goodness knows the UK needs it.Thanks again for your support. If you can continue your paid subscription and encourage others please, that will…

posts.title: How to be Bismarck's "gentleman and a half"
posts.subtitle: Basic techniques in handling people. The sooner you read "How to win friends and influence people", the sooner your success will multiply.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: They treat me like a fox, a cunning fellow of the first rank. But the truth is that with a gentleman I am always a gentleman and a half, and when I have to do with a pirate, I try to be a pirate and a half. — Otto von BismarckOne of the essential rules of politics and of life is to win by avoiding the battle if you can. If someone is willing to work with you, go the extra mile to work with them. If you can, ensure they become and remain willing to cooperate.That is where the original self-help book comes in, one of the all-time best sellers: Dale Carnegie’s How to Win Friends and Influence People. Quite possibly you don’t need to read it–maybe you are already handling people brilliantly! But I certainly found it helpful, even though I was not doing too badly at the time.How to Win Friends and Influence PeopleThe book was first recommended to me–with immense diplomacy!–by my friend Lord Syed Kamall, on a trip to Egypt in 2011 during the revolution which was then bringing much hope. I was 40. I wish I had read it in my 20s. It was itself a revolution.No one wants to be criticised or condemned. Everyone wants to be appreciated. So the first principle is “Don't criticise, condemn or complain” and the second, “Give honest and sincere appreciation.” It may seem obvious, but if so, it is not plain in everyday conduct.After the fundamental techniques, the book provides six ways to make people like you, 12 principles to win people to your way of thinking and six ways to be a leader. They are all essential reading and extremely effective: it is not for nothing that this was one of the KGB’s essential manuals1.I try…

posts.title: Free trade nation
posts.subtitle: A vision for UK trade policy on our accession to CPTPP
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: The UK has today officially joined CPTPP as a fully-fledged member– Department for Business and Trade, 15 December 2024 Promotional graphic for Plan A+, which is coming to fruitionAs I write on 15 December 2024, the UK has officially joined the Comprehensive and Progressive Trans-Pacific Partnership (CPTPP), taking GDP of members to £12 trillion. It is part of the fulfilment of a trade strategy for which I fought hard. On resigning from the Department for Exiting the EU, I insisted the ERG publish a paper on the border on the island of Ireland, the decisive issue behind the “Chequers” Brexit-in-name-only deal. That was part of a sustained campaign for legal autonomy which is well recorded elsewhere. It included supporting the work of the then Legatum Institute Special Trade Commission led by my friend Shanker Singham – here is their blueprint for UK trade policy – and then supporting Plan A+: Creating a prosperous post-Brexit UK. Along the way, frequent trade minister Greg Hands co-chaired the Alternative Arrangements Commission by Prosperity UK, setting out what could be done about our border with Ireland.At every step, we were resisted ferociously and condemned. Today, albeit with a tricky compromise over Northern Ireland, the UK is implementing the strategy which I and others could plainly state before I joined the Government in 2017. Today, it is possible for an interventionist world to enjoy free trade without anti-democratic political integration. It is a transformational success of global significance won at unnecessarily great cost. I am proud to have played my part in it.Why we left the EU: reclaiming legal autonomy and democratic control of political powerThe United Kingdom’s departure from the European Union was rooted in a profound desire to reclaim democratic control of political power and legal autonomy for the UK. The essence of…

posts.title: War minus the shooting
posts.subtitle: A practical strategy for libertarian politics, derived from a talk for Students for Liberty
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: UK Leadership in Liberty with Students for Liberty and the LSE Hayek SocietyIf war is a continuation of politics by other means, politics is war minus the shooting.On Saturday, in Ye Olde Cheshire Cheese (rebuilt 1667), I presented a strategy for practical libertarian politics to a gathering of Students for Liberty and the LSE Hayek Society. My talk was not about ideology. We discussed the challenges of advancing libertarian ideals in practical politics — where to be a libertarian is to be regarded as an alien — and I gave a strategy to make progress. I emphasised that if someone’s goal is to be regarded as a wholly consistent libertarian, they ought not to seek election.The following is derived from a transcript of my talk.A Libertarian's Reflection on PoliticsDuring 14 years in Parliament, I learnt that advancing libertarianism within a system built on state collectivist principles requires strategic discipline, pragmatism and a willingness to compromise without losing sight of ultimate goals. I reflected on my journey and shared three key lessons for libertarians entering politics.1. Purpose: Selection and Maintenance of the AimThe first and most critical lesson is to clearly define your purpose. When I entered Parliament, I knew my primary aim was to secure a referendum on the European Union. To me, democracy is defined by the opportunity to peacefully dismiss power through the ballot box. The EU, with its unelected, uncontrollable Commission which cannot be democratically replaced with other people with a different agenda, did not meet this standard. That singular focus allowed me to work strategically, dismissing distractions such as calls for me to work to liberalise firearms law, to revise drug policy or — outrageously — to overturn bans on violent pornography1. Libertarianism encompasses many worthy — and some unworthy — causes, but politics demands prioritisation.…

posts.title: A magnificent Opposition Day speech by Markus Campbell-Savours (Penrith and Solway) (Lab)
posts.subtitle: This speech by a new Labour MP representing a farming seat is a brilliant example of the pressures on an MP and the attempts of a new one to cope. Here's my analysis.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: On Wednesday, the Conservative party procured an Opposition Day debate on Farming and Inheritance Tax: watch the debate here and read Hansard here. The issue at stake is the change to inheritance tax on farms, which the Opposition has dubbed “the family farm tax”. This post is not about the policy; it is about the attempt of one new Labour MP, sitting on the Government benches and representing the farming seat of Penrith, to defend his position: Markus Campbell-Savours (Penrith and Solway) (Lab). His speech is a brilliant example of the pressures on an elected parliamentarian, pulled variously by their electors, their party and their own conscience.Here’s my analysis. Readers of a sympathetic disposition should look away now. The opening complaint: not nowI must admit that, as a new Member and a novice when it comes to the fine details of parliamentary procedure, I am surprised that we are debating this issue in this way today. As the Opposition know, the agricultural property relief measures announced in the Budget were not included in the Budget vote last month. They also know that the measures will be included in a future Finance Bill alongside other measures that will be subject to the technical consultation in the new year. This decision is simply not being made today, despite the disingenuous social media posts that imply otherwise.This opening is delicious, as someone who has been on the receiving end for many years of Labour Opposition Day debates, with their accompanying vicious and disingenuous social media, stoked ruthlessly: our brave new member reluctantly concedes his innocence and explains that the Opposition are wrong to put him in this position.In his opening, Mr Campbell-Savours is already shifting the blame: the Opposition really ought not to have brought this debate forward today, when there is so…

posts.title: We can get off the road to serfdom
posts.subtitle: Entrepreneurs will lead the way to freedom as the pace of social progress accelerates through current and new technologies which create opportunities for a free society undreamt of by our forebears.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: On Monday, we announced that I have become an advisor to Axiom, a bitcoin native corporate finance firm investing in the infrastructure of Bitcoin. I am proud further to play my part in enabling the private sector to create honest money. Read my paper for Axiom hereYears ago, I was a seed investor in Glint Pay, which has enabled all of us to “buy, save, send & spend real gold instantly”. I don’t know whether gold exchanged through fintech or Bitcoin is the money of the future – perhaps both – but I am thankful entrepreneurs are stepping up to the challenge of our age: providing honest money to the public as the present era of unsustainable spending, tax, debt and currency debasement comes to an end, inevitably in an historic crisis.My paper for Axiom revises and extends my argument set out on this substack: The system of society which is now failing is not one based on the principles of a free society, with limited government, low spending, balanced budgets and sound money. Those principles as practical political economy were casualties of the two world wars.The dominant political economy today is a technocratic belief in the omnipotence of state power. It is not working well and we cannot afford it. It has become a policy of burning the furniture to keep warm: it must end.All the problems faced by our society demand real resources. Those real resources cannot be conjured by making debt cheap. They must be produced.If our country does not step up to the task of producing more real resources than we consume, we face crisis and disaster. The sooner a government of the UK faces up boldly to that challenge, the less painful it will be.I have been making this case since my maiden speech in…

posts.title: “But why didn’t you do something about it as an MP?”
posts.subtitle: This one is for those of you thinking of seeking election: you need to know practical politics is a repeated exercise in thickening your skin.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: If you are thinking of standing for election, you will need to be tough and getting tougher. Having lost my seat, I find the latest round of skin thickening is people asking why I didn’t solve whatever issue while I was an MP. For example:“But why didn’t you do something about it as an MP?”My last article was about free speech. I closed that article with the answer in a nutshell, one of my standing jokes:I have always sought to do an infinite amount of perfect work in zero time, but I have found it necessary to make compromises.“Why didn't you do something?” is a valid criticism of course - that is why it stings.I have explained before why MPs do it: in pursuit of noble causes, like liberty. But it turns out you must pick your battles. With so many problems at hand, you cannot tackle them all: councillors, MPs, ministers and the Prime Minister must prioritise.The more general issuePublic debate can often be explained by behavioural science, as I have found in my work with Prof Paul Dolan. For example, people are hard wired for agreement in groups and will be hostile to the “out group”. People never change their minds on subjects that really matter to them. And so on.Crucially though, everyone has two patterns of thought: an intuitive “fast brain” and a slower, deliberative mind. We get through routine tasks and much of the day on our fast brain: we react to our environment and provocations. The late Daniel Kahneman explained the phenomenon in his famous book, Thinking, Fast and Slow.You see it on social media: angry, critical and often slapdash commentary which obviously won’t persuade anyone. It illustrates some advice a very wise police superintendent gave me many years ago:Society is a pressure cooker. You…

posts.title: Free speech, justice and the perils of state overreach
posts.subtitle: In a free society, the police ought to have neither the powers nor the priorities to pursue people over "non-crime hate incidents". The bar to criminal offences relating to speech must be high indeed.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Preamble — policing in WycombeIn my many years as MP for Wycombe, I always regarded the duty of any MP to be by default on the side of the police, the emergency services, the armed forces and the intelligence and security services.That faith was continuously justified by excellent local policing at all levels. The police in Wycombe were and are well-led. Officers deliver policing by consent, overwhelmingly in line with public priorities. If anything, the police have been failed by too little action to take from them the burden of being the final safety net for issues of mental health and other serious problems not primarily of offending.That is not fair on the often young and demoralised police men and women who want above all to fight crime. The police officers of my acquaintance have my admiration, respect and thanks.So it is with regret that I now lay into broader issues of police partiality and improper prioritisation, and powers they ought not to have: these are the fault of politicians and police leaders, not rank and file officers who face impossible odds every single shift.Nothing in what follows reflects on blameless officers doing their duty.Free speech is a bulwark against tyranny and a requirement for social progress.The roots of conformity and intoleranceIt is obviously undesirable for people to be rude, offensive or provocative for its own sake. Most of us prefer harmony to confrontation and quite right too. Most of us want to see law-abiding people accorded moral, legal and political equality, equal dignity and respect and equal opportunity to make the most of their lives. There is nothing admirable about saying things which are wrong, offensive or hateful.Alas, people not only disagree but, as I have increasingly discovered during my work with Professor Paul Dolan:The human condition is wired…

posts.title: Chums - How a Tiny Caste of Oxford Tories Took Over the UK
posts.subtitle: Chums is a chippy, bitter, cynical and partial account of the UK’s Eton- and Oxford-educated ruling class. But it is highly entertaining and thoroughly recommended. And to hell with class conflict.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: I have waited a while to read Chums, which squarely blames Brexit on an Oxford-educated clique comprising David Cameron, Boris Johnson, Michael Gove, Daniel Hannan and Dominic Cummings, amongst others. I may have fallen for confirmation bias, but I found it elaborated much which I knew to be true about the aliens in whose company I walked for 14 years. Men – overwhelmingly – who have regarded me as likewise alien. People who seem to live by an unwritten and long-assumed set of rules: rules mostly about hierarchy, what really matters and how to behave. They are rules unknown to those of us who went to state school and I have often been happy to bulldoze through them.I grew up in a working-class community in Cornwall in the 70s and 80s. My father was a lifelong building site carpenter before he retired. My mother was an accounting clerk and cleaner who rose to run a significant local charity for the disabled. After the divorce, my stepfather was an open-cast mineworker. Grandad was a toolmaker - and we thought that a higher occupation Sir Keir. I went to the local primary school and comprehensive, with a year out in a prep school on the maximum scholarship (I was thought too bright to be state educated, believe it or not). It was not a hard childhood, apart from the emotional consequences of the divorce, but it was a world away from the privilege and entitlement described in this book and which I have long witnessed.It would be easy to grow a chip like the author’s. I don’t think I have: over the years, I regarded the old Etonian paternalists as sometime allies and sometime obstacles to be bulldozed through in active disregard of their unspoken conventions. Sometimes I felt examined as if…

posts.title: President Trump.
posts.subtitle: It’s not all bad.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Over on his Substack, Jacob makes a positive case for Trump. I am not so sanguine.Even on Jacob’s central point – that “Donald Trump has succeeded in blowing up the liberal elite” – I take no pleasure in this kind of defeat of people I too oppose. After all, I am putting quite a bit of time and effort into fostering higher quality disagreements. I don’t want a world with so much hostility in the face of disagreement: we ought to disagree more gracefully. And beat our opponents – the opponents of a free society – more roundly and finally.My discomfort with President Trump is not personal. His criminal conviction in May for attempting to cover up a hush money payment to pornstar Stormy Daniels ought to rule him out firmly. This is not mere prudishness: those who wield power ought to have integrity in all our interests. It is among the principles we concede at our peril.Trump threatens the preservation of democratic norms, standards which are instituted to ensure the proper functioning of the main purpose of democracy: the transfer of power without violence. Trump’s attacks on the press, judiciary and other institutions are potentially dangerous even if those institutions are in sore need of reform. How such things are approached matters if the peace is to be kept through faith in institutions.I dislike Trump’s divisive style. Politics is war minus the shooting, which makes it more imperative not less that political leaders pull their punches and create room for the constructive functioning of social relations despite disagreement. It was particularly culpable when he did not condemn extremists.Trump’s economic policy is susceptible to criticism, often because it is misunderstood. I see his trade policy for example as not so much protectionist but defensive against the massive distortions which other…

posts.title: Was it worth it?
posts.subtitle: On this day 17 years ago, I decided to stand for election; 15 years ago today, I was selected as the Conservative candidate for Wycombe at my first attempt. Much has happened. Was it worth it?
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: In 2007, I was so infuriated by politics and politicians, I asked what I could do. I decided I could emigrate, moan fruitlessly or stand for election to the Commons.With nowhere worth emigrating to and no desire to be bitter, I decided to seek election, with no prior experience or connections. I wanted to be sure I could count on at least one vote in Parliament. It was not a small decision: it meant abandoning my plans to found a software company in the City, amongst other things. Was it worth it?Two years later to the day, at my first attempt, I was selected as the Conservative candidate for Wycombe. Fifteen years on, was it worth it?The issuesThe issues which motivated me had a common theme: a collapse in commitment to the principles of a free society for which I thought I had joined the Royal Air Force during the Cold War. Then as now, I thought some ideas mattered so much they were worth fighting for, even with lethal force. Even if it meant dying for the sake of others.Ideas like democracy, the capacity of the public to remove and replace those in power peacefully at the ballot box, freedom of expression and freedom from being killed unjustly by the state. Quite important ideas around which I thought everyone could and would rally.War and counter-terrorismI was at the Cheesewring on Bodmin Moor, Cornwall with my mother when the planes struck the Twin Towers on 9/11. It was horrifying. I doubt any of us will forget where we were. I had left the RAF for the private sector. Beth was in the Middle East on a detachment, where she was immediately locked down.Notwithstanding the horror, I thought the wars in Afghanistan and Iraq were wrong. As Labour’s response to terrorism…

posts.title: Leadership: vision, values and truth
posts.subtitle: With thanks for the feedback on the last post, here's what people asked for.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: I am a Conservative to preserve all that is good in our constitution, a Radical to remove all that is bad. I seek to preserve property and to respect order, and I equally decry the appeal to the passions of the many or the prejudices of the few.Benjamin Disraeli, twice Conservative Prime Minister, in a campaign speech at High Wycombe in 1832In my last article, I set out the leadership the Conservative party and the country needs. I focussed on basic leadership models, transformational “Level 5” leadership and the elements of trust. Readers asked me to say more about vision, values and truth.Thank you for feedback on my last articleVision and valuesRepeat after me, “lower taxes”.So said my first employer on leaving the Royal Air Force, when he learned I intended to stand as a Conservative candidate. He was right: lower taxes are among the keys to a freer society, one more prosperous and happier. On my website, I long ago stated where I stand, like Disraeli both Conservative and Radical:In five principles:Peace with a strong defence.Family as the building block of society, not the state.Equality before the law.Freedom from arbitrary government – the classical Rule of Law.Property – the unity of ownership, control and risk bearing.As an engineer, I am pragmatic but I recognise the importance of having good ideas, even if they can only be applied imperfectly. […]I am concerned with the alignment of ends and means. If the end we have chosen is a healthy society in which people are prosperous, secure and free then we had better foster positive relationships, produce sufficient real wealth and create an environment of responsible liberty with the best possible systems of healthcare and education.I believe that every person is valuable and that every person who can should produce something of…

posts.title: The leadership the Conservative party needs
posts.subtitle: Humility, resolve, positive relations, professional competence and consistency. That is, a break from the norm.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: 2024 Conservative leadership contenders Kemi Badenoch and Robert JenrickThe continual theme of my 32 year career has been leadership. I have no doubt I have made mistakes, especially as a 20-something Royal Air Force engineer officer managing older and more experienced experts, thrown in at the deep end. Doubtless in software, politics and government too. But somehow, my projects seem to succeed, often notoriously so. Organising people for a purpose is what I do.Political leadership is in a different category to leadership in the armed forces or business. Members of the Armed Forces choose to be under lawful authority: they are commanded and they are required to obey lawful commands. Employees in business can meet the reasonable requirements of their employer or move on and be replaced.In neither business nor the armed forces can the followers remove the leader. Authority is given and if you don’t like how it is exercised, there is not much you can do about it.However, in democratic politics – especially in the Conservative party – the followers very much can remove the leader and, as we have seen, do. Moreover, the followers are themselves sustained in office by their electors and party selectors: the pyramid of power is inverted. All that means the leadership we require is at a higher standard, not least because it imposes additional complexity in an exceptionally large and complicated context.Basic leadership modelsIn a complicated world, there is more demand for leaders than can be met with natural supply. Among the organisations which therefore attempt to create leaders, the Royal Air Force teaches the functional approach to leadership, particularly following John Adair. Alas I have lost the card which divided up the three functions of task, team and individual into checklists providing an aide memoire. Depending on circumstances, the leader will…

posts.title: I’m glad to back Kemi.
posts.subtitle: It’s time for authentic belief in ideas that work.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Rarely is anyone ready to lead a political party, still less become Prime Minister, as Keir Starmer is busy illustrating. No perfect candidate is ever available: we must choose from available options.I will back Kemi Badenoch to lead the Conservative Party and with a good heart too.It is true Kemi has sometimes picked fights for which she was not prepared. Developing greater restraint and control in interviews is a choice and one she will have to make. There is time.I don’t have a bad word for Robert. He is a professional and serious man, making his case robustly. I was somewhat disappointed by his intervention on special forces, but anyone can make a mistake. Anyone is entitled to learn and update their views. I am sure I have.But what marks Kemi apart is her authentic and ferocious belief in solid Conservative ideas. That has been lacking for far too long: without an intellectual keel, the ship of state is tossed about in the storm of events. Any leader must adjust, adapt and compromise but they ought always to be brought back on course by their values and ideas. It seems most likely Kemi will be.That is my bet: that Kemi Badenoch will find the balance necessary to put her authentic belief in ideas that work into practice for the benefit of us all.

posts.title: What the next Conservative leader faces
posts.subtitle: A popularity contest is not enough: government spending is forecast to soar beyond revenues. The next Tory leader has an historic task ahead: balancing the nation's books, likely in deep crisis.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: The Conservative leadership contest has been shallow: a popularity contest of vague but attractive ambitions for what should be achieved and why, without any serious substance about how. From war to our response to climate change, criminal justice to poverty fighting, we face difficult and sometimes existential risks and problems. The task of securing peace, prosperity and social progress is an immense challenge, one which requires real resources to back imagination, vision, humility and resolve.In this post, I will set out how severe the challenge is, using authoritative facts plus the best projections we have. It is a case I have made many times over years.Soaring spending plus stagnant revenuesThe most recent fiscal risks and sustainability report from the Office for Budget Responsibility provides a stark illustration of spending soaring beyond revenues:Our problem in a nutshellThey articulate clearly the future pressures we face: an ageing population, climate change and geopolitical tensions. The public finances are on an unsustainable path and governments will need to take “mitigating policy action” to prevent that debt spiral occurring. Our problem in a nutshell: debtThat is a euphemism for deep cuts where they hurt most: pensions, healthcare, education, social care. It would be a time of extraordinary suffering, a time difficult to reconcile with the pressures of democracy. This nightmare can be seen to be crystallising now in a general state of decay and failure. There is reason to hope, but it relies on leadership with a firm grasp of reality, a clear plan and the skill to carry it through.Hope: productivityThankfully, those debt projections are highly sensitive to productivity gains.Hope: productivity gainsWith the age of AI dawning plus the potential of fusion power always just over the horizon, genetics informing medicine and robotics becoming more accessible, it is conceivable people are about to produce…

posts.title: The problem with Nigel Farage
posts.subtitle: Reform is a mistake.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Our aim is not to reach a compromise with the Conservatives. It is to replace them as the real opposition to a socialist Labour Party that is in trouble already.Real politics is difficult.Writing in the Telegraph, Nigel Farage has said he will not do a deal with the Conservative Party. I am not surprised: together with a senior businessman, it was me who initiated and progressed conversations with Nigel and Richard Tice in 2019 towards their decision not to stand Brexit Party candidates against Conservatives: they understood it would defeat their own political ends to do so but the decision came at a high price.Today, they take a different view. Nigel writes,Reform is here to stay. The Tories had their chance and they blew it. All talk of whether a future deal between me and the Conservatives can be done is irrelevant. It is not even on my agenda, I simply don’t trust them.Alas. Particularly when Jacob Rees-Mogg has been working hard to unite the right.The problem with Nigel FarageI am told Nigel and I disagree about little of substance. I don’t know about that. I do know that I dislike intensely how he communicates and the way he alienates people: it is not merely ill-mannered to cause unnecessary offence, it is potentially dangerous. When people have a big voice in the public square, what they say and how matters.For that reason, we have only been in touch rarely. There’s no animosity from my point of view: however influential Nigel is, I want a political discourse capable of embracing difference and that is not his approach. I don’t want to work with him.What is true of me is true of many decent centre-right voters: they just don’t want politics done that way. This is Nigel’s big problem: he repels too…

posts.title: It's a good time to be a Conservative
posts.subtitle: My remarks at the IEA Late Night Free Spirits Reception yesterday.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Last night, I was honoured to speak to the IEA’s Late Night Free Spirits Reception. I gave five reasons why this is a good time to be a Conservative.There are five reasons this is a good time to be a Conservative. It is not because we like opposition but because we were never going to win a fifth term. It would have been extraordinary to win another majority: the question was how badly we would lose. We did not need to lose this badly, but this loss of power was expected.If Carlsberg made opponents… In power, Labour is a circus act, guilty of all they threw at us, on steroids. As former Labour MP Rosie Duffield wrote, resigning the whip, “The sleaze, nepotism and apparent avarice are off the scale.” Of course she has long-standing grievances with her leadership, but anyone can see what she wrote is true: Labour are in collapse, already. It is no wonder everyone who attended tells me Labour’s conference was a sad and downbeat affair. There is talk of Starmer being removed. There is no resetting this mess. And the ambitious new princes and princesses of the Labour party will soon realise they can’t all be in Cabinet, at which point chaos will begin to ensue via WhatsApp and social media. Alas this will probably grind on for several years, leaving the country in a worse place, but a place from which a resolute and thoughtful Conservative party could do the right thing, as it usually does after trying everything else.Labour have succeeded in a broad but shallow victory which can be relatively easily reversed by competent politicians. In Wycombe, Labour’s vote fell while mine collapsed. The LibDems lost voters and Reform only returned to about UKIP’s vote of 2015. This seems broadly representative. This…

posts.title: I don't like politics but I want to make a difference
posts.subtitle: Understanding parliamentary politics: party membership matters.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: As an engineer, I believe in the practical application of robust ideas.When you go on holiday, sitting in the plane, you want to believe the airworthiness engineers knew what they were doing. When it breaks down just before take off, you want someone to turn up and fix it fast. This is what engineers face: know what you are doing but get on with it, compromising within bounds to get the job done efficiently, effectively and safely.That’s why, when I came into politics, I read and read and read.One of the more practical books was I don’t like politics but I want to make a difference by John Redwood, 2 October 2006. I commend it to you.I don’t like politics but I want to make a difference by John Redwood, 2 October 2006.From the summary on Amazon:The past decade has seen a sharp decline in membership of political parties, with a severe drop in those who vote in local and general elections. Voters are disillusioned by spin and 'Punch and Judy' politics. There is cynicism and a breakdown in trust that Westminster can really make a difference.
