But millions back campaigns for trade justice and against world poverty, attend meetings and protest about environmental issues. They are deeply concerned about nuclear and renewable energy, about the transfer of power to quangos and Brussels; they seek to defend free speech and freedom from the abuse of power, and are often passionate about local issues that matter to them. John Redwood targets those who believe that they are apolitical, but who are genuinely concerned about issues which they do not see as 'politics'. He tells main parties they need to change, to make themselves more relevant to the modern fancy-free and passionate electorate. He addresses the public's concerns and suggests how the…

posts.title: Why do MPs do it?
posts.subtitle: Understanding what motivates MPs, better to help them to help you.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: With apologies to my former colleague Edward Timpson, typical embellishment of a political sign.The life of an MP is an all-consuming battle to engage the disengaged, mollify the furious and demanding, and serve effectively the vulnerable. Without thanks but with fierce condemnation and the constant allegation that you don’t really care about wherever. It may be well-paid indoor work without heavy lifting, but you might reasonably ask why MPs want to do it.The publicPerfectly reasonably - and thankfully! - the vast majority of the public are, most of the time, rationally disinterested in politics and their politicians. Why would they take the time to follow the business of the Commons or the vote-seeking antics of their MP on social media?1After all, they have lives to be getting on with.I vividly remember a scene in the 2010 election. I could take you to the house on Desborough Avenue. I knocked. No one was in: the most common response. I turned and in pulled the family car, plainly containing Mum, Grandma and the kids.“Hello Mrs [X],” I said, “I’m Steve Baker, your Conservative parliamentary candidate. I called to see if you had any issues or concerns to discuss.”“Let’s see,” she replied, cheerily. “Mum’s just had a good hospital appointment. The kids are doing well in good schools. My husband has a great job and we can afford the mortgage. And we are going on holiday soon. So everything is alright thank you.”That’s life. Not the obsessions of politicians and political activists. And thank goodness. Until something goes wrong.At any moment, the vast majority of the public are not writing to their MP; MPs would certainly not cope if they did. A small number - probably 100-200 - who will never vote for the Conservative incumbent are creating about 80% of the correspondence…

posts.title: The Conservative party is the best hope we have for good government.
posts.subtitle: The Conservative party has taken a beating, and rightly so. There are signs it is moving towards its centrist dad comfort zone: mere conservatism. But I am sticking with it.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: The Conservative Party is the least disgusting political party in the world.So said a libertarian academic of my acquaintance at an event not long after I was elected. We got along famously.The 1982 Conservative Party logo, via logos.fandom.comPolitical ideasThere are fundamentally three political economies by which we can be governed in a democracy:Liberalism of the old kind, which seeks the development of social progress and prosperity through peace and liberty under the rule of law. Social democracy which pursues egalitarianism through state power. Mere conservatism, which seeks to preserve tradition and avoid change, sceptical as it is about the capacity of society to improve.Occasionally in history, the idea arises of extreme authoritarian collectivism. This may be full-blooded socialism – which was once a synonym for communism – or fascism of one kind or another. Communism and fascism are rightly regarded as opposite sides of the same totalitarian coin, characterised by suppression of individual rights and by the rise of the total state. Militant, militaristic, nationalistic and authoritarian, these regimes are the outworking of the worst sickness of the human condition.Reagan said in 1964,You and I are told increasingly we have to choose between a left or right. Well I'd like to suggest there is no such thing as a left or right. There's only an up or down - [up to] man's old-aged dream, the ultimate in individual freedom consistent with law and order, or down to the ant heap of totalitarianism. And regardless of their sincerity, their humanitarian motives, those who would trade our freedom for security have embarked on this downward course.And it is no wonder he said it in 1964, within recent memory of World War Two and during the Cold War. Once only needs to look at the history of state spending in the UK to…

posts.title: A complete triumph for Sir Keir Starmer
posts.subtitle: Yesterday's votes on pensioners' Winter Fuel Payments were a complete triumph for the Labour PM. Or he is a blundering oaf. Alas it seems more likely the Conservatives leapt into a tar trap.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: The Labour government means-testing pensioners’ Winter Fuel Payments created an obvious and easy opportunity for the Opposition to cause immense pain to Labour MPs. Some, like Rachel Reeves, heaped scorn and condemnation on the Coalition government of 2010-15 as we cleared up their mess: the temptation to savour schadenfreude and take revenge will have been great indeed, and remains so. One only needs to check Conservative and Reform MPs’ social media to see they have seized with energy and enthusiasm the opportunity offered. It is a mistake, but one they were inevitably going to make and which was no doubt foreseen.Unfortunately, unless Starmer and Reeves are complete incompetents who have stumbled into accepting daft civil service advice and created an inadvertent controversy, it seems likely the Conservatives and Reform have blundered.I am convinced my former colleagues have leapt into a tar pit dug for them, one in which they will now sink and be driven deeper and from which they will struggle to escape. I warned this sort of thing would happen. Alas.Any fool can take things at face value. A strategic Opposition ought to think deeply and act with purpose and resolve.What happened yesterdayOn the order paper yesterday were two relevant debates: a motion to annul the delegated legislation which implements the change and an opposition day debate to further discuss the issue. The first debate and vote was about whether the law would go through. The second was important politics.The Opposition Day motion takes some reading but it contains three important indicators of what is afoot:That this House regrets that the Government approved the use of the urgency exemption in section 173 of the Social Security Administration Act 1992 to make and lay the Social Fund Winter Fuel Payment Regulations 2024 before the Secretary of State had referred…

posts.title: Leaders can't command loyalty
posts.subtitle: Interview with Andrew Marr on LBC after round 1 of the 2024 Conservative leadership contest in which Priti Patel was knocked out.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: On Wednesday 4 September, Conservative MPs voted in the first round of their leadership contest. Priti Patel was knocked out.Andrew Marr kindly invited me to join him on LBC with their Political Correspondent, Agnes Chambre. The full interview is above and the key points and analysis below.The processThe first round of the Tory leadership campaign unexpectedly saw Mel Stride survive, while Priti Patel was ousted. Robert Jenrick leads with 28 votes, followed by Kemi Badenoch with 27, and James Cleverly with 26. The votes are close and strategic manoeuvring among candidates is to be expected. Four candidates will go ahead to Conservative conference to be assessed by members, which is likely to change the dynamic. Supporters of the leading candidates and Priti Patel are likely to assess who they wish to knock out. Candidates appear to be emphasising a move to the common ground with the public, rather than to a chimerical “centre ground” or “right”, particularly on immigration and Brexit. The final decision of members from among MPs’ final two will be announced on November 2nd.Attempting to “command” loyalty from MPs is futileA challenge all candidates face is uniting the Conservative party in Parliament. Of course the whole party and the country must be led, but the first challenge is to secure discipline at least from members of parliament. In a previous post, I set out how to unite the Conservative Party in Parliament. The corollary is that leaders cannot expect to command unity through force of personality or by their record. Alas that no candidate appears to have set out how they would unite and keep united the MPs they wish to lead. James Cleverly has said:To command loyalty you need to have displayed loyalty. And I have always served the leader of the day, never briefed against…

posts.title: What do MPs do?
posts.subtitle: Find you a home? Get you off parking fines? Manage the NHS? The role of an MP is frequently misunderstood and it is all our fault. Part of a series on understanding parliamentary politics.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Society is a pressure cooker. You are the safety release valve. So said a very wise police superintendent soon after I was elected. And he was right. But what do MPs actually do? Conservative MPs in the House of Commons, before the 2024 electionJudging by the problems brought to me by constituents over 14 years, members of the public believe MPs:Allocate housing,Process immigration cases,Appeal benefit decisions,Find school places,Overturn court decisions,Get the bins emptied,Plan housing development,Get you off parking tickets and speeding fines,Call off the police when you are committing criminal offences, andHelp you to - and I am not kidding - return unwanted parcels.This is a tiny selection of the things brought to me and my team as an MP. None of them is the job of an MP, even though trying to help across a wide field is rightly routine when the system fails and people are in distress. I am grateful to my caseworkers that we achieved so much for local people in unlocking bureaucracy and correcting injustice. And yet the functions I list are not the responsibility of an MP.If you listened to local political activists, you might add to the list:Managing the NHS,Directing schools how to run themselves,Telling the police their priorities,Conducting foreign policy, andInfluencing legal processes in other countries.Again, none of these things is the responsibility of an MP or within the power of a back-bench MP. They are mostly not functions even of ministers: conducting foreign policy is the exception.This post is about what MPs are for and what they do. Once again, I aim to be astonishingly frank to assist readers to understand the fundamental forces driving politics and public policy.What is an MP for?My former colleague James Gray MP once wrote a great extended article “What’s an MP for?” By permission of…

posts.title: The Principles for a Free Society
posts.subtitle: We are fighting for a free future - but what does that mean? Twelve principles every citizen could reasonably demand from politicians, journalists and public figures.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: “Among the laws that rule human societies there is one which seems to be more precise and clear than all others. If men are to remain civilised or to become so, the art of associating together must grow and improve in the same ratio in which the equality of conditions is increased.” – Alexis de TocquevillePrinciples for a Free Society, © 2003 Dr Nigel Ashford and the Jarl Hjalmarson Foundation - get the PDFA concerned citizen might reasonably ask today why the world is not working as well as we would wish. It was a question I began seriously asking in about 2006.I started reading elsewhere, but if I had known about Principles for a Free Society by Dr Nigel Ashford for the Jarl Hjalmarson Foundation, I would have started here. In just 92 pages, the book presents 12 principles with unrivalled clarity and brevity. The book was the foundation of the book club I ran in Parliament.The principles are:Civil societyDemocracyEqualityFree enterpriseFreedomHuman rightsJusticePeacePrivate propertyThe rule of lawSpontaneous orderTolerationI strongly recommend the book but I will highlight the two principles about which I believe we have the most trouble today.EqualityMisconceptions about equality are at the heart of many of our difficulties. The pursuit of a kind of cosmic justice which accounts for past wrongs and yet provides equal outcomes today is at the heart of much muddled, damaging and dangerous political thought and social policy. Ashford explains:Equality is the principle that people should be treated the same or equally. Yet people are obviously not the same or ‘equal’ in most of their characteristics, in talents, abilities, looks or preferences. No one believes that every human being is the same, so in what sense are people equal? The debate about equality is about when it is, and is not, right to treat…

posts.title: A huge thank you to all my subscribers!
posts.subtitle: I have been blown away by your support - thank you. I have applied 7 days comp to everyone, which will extend existing paid subscriptions
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: When I started this Substack, I had no expectations for how it would grow.Today, over 700 people have subscribed, with more paying subscribers than I could have imagined - thank you! I am blown away by your support.I have applied 7 days complimentary access to everyone. Existing paid subscribers will be extended 7 days.Your feedback on my writing so far, and suggestions for what would be most helpful to you in future, would be very welcome.Thank you!StevePhoto by Morvanic Lee on Unsplash

posts.title: Power corrupts
posts.subtitle: Influence and authority are earned at the price of consent and compromise. Corruption of principle and the appearance of it are evident in actions and outcomes. What is to be done?
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Labour recently ended universal winter fuel payments, restricting them to only those receiving means-tested benefits. There has been outcry. All the Conservative Leadership candidates want lower taxes and a smaller state, but also oppose the change, despite the unavoidable reality of default if we do not cut age-related spending and focus support on those who need it most. The position is absurd - and damaging. Conservatives should support every spending cut and oppose every tax rise if we are to eventually get this country on the sustainable financial path we need. My advice was to give Labour the kiss of death but all our potential leaders surrendered what is necessary and right to what is easy. Why? To win.Alas that power corrupts. In this, the first in a series on how politics and parliament works, I explain how.Power corruptsPower tends to corrupt, and absolute power corrupts absolutelyThe famous political writer Lord Acton argued that power tends to corrupt and absolute power corrupts absolutely. Yet today, issues among MPs are rare, minor by international standards and carry consequences. Good. It means our institutions are working.Acton’s argument was that individuals with power would prioritise personal gain over ethical considerations. With a lack of accountability and transparency, people succumb to temptation.That is why our institutions enforce transparency, accountability, standards and consequences. The UK scores well on the Corruption Perceptions Index and the Legatum Prosperity Index. MPs pay, pensions and business costs are decided, regulated and administered independently.The problem at hand is not corrupt politicians enriching themselves but politicians surrendering their principles, or seeming to, with all that entails.How politicians come to surrender their principlesBack in 2006-7, I was appalled by the conduct of the then Labour government and the inadequacies of the Conservative party. What could I do? Emigrate, moan or stand. I…

posts.title: Racism is running riot
posts.subtitle: There is never any excuse for rioting and racism. It is also true that large numbers of people have legitimate grievances which have not been adequately addressed. A distinction must be drawn.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: The planned far right protest in Wycombe was a damp squib. I was glad but not surprised: Wycombe is exceptionally diverse and also well-integrated. Any town has problems, but in ours, people rubbing along peacefully is the well-established norm.High Wycombe town centreMeanwhile, the PM condemns 'far-right thuggery. He is right that there is no excuse for rioting: there never is. Yet you would not believe it from some commentators: in their rage at vile crimes against innocent children, some are confusing legitimate grievances with illegitimate action. People with public voices ought to do better, especially politicians.Subscribe nowThe situationWe can all read the news. These riots are a response - a completely wrong response - to appalling, high profile violent offences which every decent person utterly condemns. Unfortunately, there have been a range of inflammatory responses, including speculation about the identity of perpetrators.Some people I would normally expect to admire have said some out of character and, it seems to me, quite dangerous things. The essence is the same: the suggestion that these riots are an inevitable consequence of elite failure on immigration and integration policy. I watched in horror as one TV presenter said the authorities, the state and the law would not protect people and that the public were on their own: this was grossly reckless and inflammatory.Parliament is the place for the peaceful airing and resolution of the public’s grievances: as a wise police superintendent once told me, “Society is a pressure cooker. You are the safety release valve.” And he was right. And when mass legitimate grievances are not addressed, trouble inevitably follows that failure of democracy.Yes, there have been failures and yes, legitimate grievances have gone unaddressed. The burden now falls on MPs to show they are listening and addressing those legitimate grievances.However, seeming to excuse…

posts.title: How to unite the Conservative party in Parliament
posts.subtitle: Calling for unity to win is never enough. Give MPs what they want: influence and a shared purpose. The same is true of any party.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: For better or worse and always thinking it in a just cause, between 2015 and 2022, working with others, I led some of the biggest and most effective parliamentary insurgencies in history. So I was not surprised when journalists asked for my opinion of this new Labour Growth Group.I replied to one that it is plainly a put-up job by Labour’s No10: you can tell by the way it is calling for delivery of the manifesto and that it is led by a parliamentary private secretary. It is comical anyone is expected to believe this is some kind of rebellion.But you can see why anyone would ask: divisions among MPs of the same party and “ginger groups” in pursuit of one policy or another have a long history in the Commons, stretching way back before technology enabled rebellions of the scale I orchestrated. Consider the groups No Turning Back, The 92 Group, Cornerstone, One Nation, Tory Reform Group and so on.No wonder every Conservative leadership contender is setting out to unite the party to win. But is repeating, “Divided parties don’t win elections!” enough?No. It has not worked in the past and it will not work in the future. To understand why, and what to do instead, you need to understand how MPs tick. The Conservative Parliamentary Party, January 2024What Conservative leaders and whips always sayLeaders and whips call reliably for unity because divided parties do not win. This is both superficially persuasive and plainly untrue.It is superficially persuasive because it stands to reason: of course the public want to vote for a united, professional party with a common cause who, always getting things right, never seek to sound off in the Commons or the press. Obviously, that should be the ambition, but after one of the most bitterly divided…

posts.title: The Future of the Right
posts.subtitle: If the Conservative party does not stand for freedom, it stands for nothing. The recent IEA panel and the challenge now facing those of us who are fighting for a free future.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: The Institute of Economic Affairs was good enough to invite me to speak as part of an august panel, The Future of the Right, on 24 July. You can watch all my remarks, throughout the event, thanks to the IEA on YouTube. This article directly answers the key questions posed in framing the event and then articulates the task facing the Right.If you support the work of the IEA, please subscribe to IEA Insider now.IEA panel discussion - the Future of the RightThe IEA’s questionsHow could we define ‘the right’ of UK politics?At the extreme, the Right is taken to include authoritarian collectivists, driven by militant nationalism, isolationism, nativism and protectionism. It is an ugly and awful form of state collectivism which I utterly oppose and which I very much hope the UK will continue to reject. No UK think tank of which I am aware stands for that Right.I prefer to think not of a straight line between left and right: bring the extremes around to make a circle so that communism and fascism are the left and right sides of the same totalitarian coin, diametrically opposed to classical liberalism. That recasts politics as Reagan characterised it in A Time for Choosing:You and I are told increasingly we have to choose between a left or right. Well I'd like to suggest there is no such thing as a left or right. There's only an up or down - [up to] man's [age-old] dream, the ultimate in individual freedom consistent with law and order, or down to the ant heap of totalitarianism. And regardless of their sincerity, their humanitarian motives, those who would trade our freedom for security have embarked on this downward course.This is the proper and useful definition of the healthy Right: those who believe in making progress…

posts.title: Rachel Reeves first major misstep
posts.subtitle: If the Chancellor tells us the UK can't afford public spending, she is right. The Conservatives should use the opportunity to promote lasting change, better to serve the public and avoid disaster.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor of the Exchequer, is expected on Monday to give a statement on public spending “pressures” amounting to tens of billions of pounds. It is not credible to suggest this will be a surprise, as Paul Johnson of the IFS explained on the Today programme this morning (07:37).Either it will be a restatement of what is already known – in which case any claim this is a surprise is frankly dishonest - or it will be a wish list of spending which government departments would really like and may have bid for in spending reviews – in which case elements will range from the unaffordable to the fanciful. Reeves is not at all likely to raise taxes on Monday but instead to set the scene for rises later.It is Rachel Reeves first major misstep. Not because the Labour party promised not to raise taxes on working people: this early in the parliament, as politicians so often do (alas), they may simply break their promises, blaming their predecessors. It is a major misstep because with taxes at historic highs, and likely at or beyond Britain’s taxable capacity, Reeves would be telling us an unspoken truth which undermines her party’s faith in big government: that the UK cannot afford the state we have. She would be right and the Conservatives should point that out. Atlas is shrugging.Subscribe nowAtlas is shrugging: the public cannot afford the burden of the state placed upon them and on which so many are dependent: not now and not into the future. A time for choosing is fast approaching, probably at the next general election, once Labour have further tested the limits of their ideology of the big, entrepreneurial state.With more people living longer and so many people dependent on the state for pensions and…

posts.title: Power and society
posts.subtitle: In defence of Christian Libertarianism
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: I have a confession: I am not a conservative. My politics are what is known as Christian Libertarian. A while ago, I was asked to defend my ideas in Parliament to a group of centre-right Christians in politics, public life and the clergy. It follows below.I received two criticisms. At the time, one theologian rightly corrected my pronunciation of the Greek word "storge". Subsequently, a local vicar responded that I should have covered justice: it is a fair point. I think I would survey Rawls and Nozick, combining them to say God’s justice is that you get what you deserve: both the consequences of your actions and a basic minimum beneath which a civilised society should allow no one to fall.As usual before addressing matters of faith, I am clear that the use of power can only ever be legitimately justified by evidence and reason.If you enjoy this speech or find it useful, you may also like my article In Defence of Capitali$m | a Christian perspective for BMS World Mission.This is a speech about power and love as alternative organising principles of human relations. It is a speech about society and politics, about God and government.It is not a Conservative speech, nor of course socialist, but one radically liberal in the old tradition, a tradition of freedom which arguably was and is only possible under the conditions of the Christian faith.The fundamental point of this speech is this: the big story of the Bible - the reason Christ came and will come again - is that power, expressed in the law, will never set society right, not for God and certainly not for us, and not if free will is to have any meaning.Subscribe nowI will not argue that God’s law is bad, nor that human law is bad.…

posts.title: The Kiss of Death
posts.subtitle: How the Conservatives should respond to Labour’s first King’s Speech
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: As I set out in my last post, Labour aim to become the natural party of Government by manoeuvring to the free market right of the Conservatives. The Conservatives must now respond to today’s King’s Speech with a Kiss of Death strategy: they must support Labour’s rightward move, encouraging them to go further while condemning them for 14 years of wasted hard left Opposition. Opposition which held the Overton window of plausible policy in a position of failure, much to the detriment of the public. Only that way can the Conservatives avoid destruction and begin to fracture an apparently unassailable Labour party in the Commons.Subscribe nowHousing and healthcare are the key issues at hand. Starmer will probably renationalise the railways as a sop to the left. All three issues risk the Conservative party instinctively moving left. I will return to the railways.After 14 years of Conservative government, far too many people are locked out of housing, a foundation of a full, prosperous family life. Too many young people despair of home ownership and despair at the crippling burden of rent: the country must urgently build homes which the young can hope to afford. With no right to say no and no incentive to acquiesce, Conservative home owners will often be up in arms.At no stage have the Conservatives been trusted by the electorate with the human services of health, welfare and education. So, instead of modernising them, the Conservatives showered them with money to win electoral favour. It was a strategy that worked for a long time, in combination with education reforms of which Conservatives can be proud but which were not mirrored in health and which have disappointed in welfare.That approach has now run onto the rocks as the money runs out. I will return in my next post…

posts.title: The King's Speech and Labour's strategy
posts.subtitle: Analysis of Labour’s strategy, which frames the coming King's Speech, by my Risk and Opportunity group
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Subscribe nowThe State Opening of Parliament will take place on Wednesday 17 July, hearing the first King’s Speech of this Labour Government.That Gracious Speech will only be properly understood through the lens of Labour’s strategy. So today I’m publishing my paper reported by the Telegraph in December:Minister warns Rishi Sunak that Labour is outflanking Tories on the RightMinister warns Rishi Sunak that Labour is outflanking Tories on the Right, TelegraphLabour aim to become the natural party of Government by manoeuvring to the free market right of the Conservatives. I will return in September with more analysis of their strategy, tactics and progress. In the meantime, the following is the text of a paper prepared by my Risk and Opportunity Group of advisers.Subscribe nowRisk and Opportunity: Analysis of Labour’s StrategyBackgroundPrior to Blair’s election as Prime Minister in 1997, Britain had undergone significant change.Following the devaluation of the pound in 1967, the IMF loan of 1976, and the Winter of Discontent in 1978-9, Margaret Thatcher had become Prime Minister in 1979. Enacting a market oriented agenda, the Conservatives were trusted by the electorate to control public expenditure whilst opening up British industry to more competitive global forces.It is against this backdrop that between 1979 and 1997 the Thatcher and Major administrations promoted varying degrees of privatisation, deregulation and contracting out of services whilst they also abandoned exchange controls and significantly reduced the marginal rate of income tax.However, at no stage were the Conservatives trusted by the electorate with the human services of health, welfare, and education. So, instead of modernising them, the Conservatives instead showered them with money to win electoral favour. He discusses the future Tory leader, the rise of Nigel Farage, and why he’s the man that ‘knows how it’s done.’– SpectatorTVDiscover more about The 3 Elements of Trust at the Harvard Business Review:As a leader, you want the people in your organization to trust you. And with good reason. In our coaching with leaders, we often see that trust is a leading indicator of whether others evaluate them positively or negatively. By analyzing over 80,000 360-degree reviews, the authors found that there are three elements that predict whether a leader will be trusted by his direct reports, peers, and other colleagues. These are positive relationships, consistency, and good judgment/expertise. […]

posts.title: Coming soon
posts.subtitle: What to expect from my substack: analysis of and insight into the forces driving the political events of the day.
posts.author: Steve Baker
posts.text: Subscribe nowThe situationPolitics is in turmoil across Europe and North America. In the UK, Labour just won a “loveless landslide” with a majority of 180 yet “The gap between the share of total votes won by the winning party in the 2024 general election and the share of Parliamentary seats won is the largest on record”.The state of the parties, via parliament.ukIn the tsunami, I was swept away as Wycombe’s MP. While the number of votes cast for Labour fell significantly, the number of Conservative votes cast collapsed as about half of previous Conservative voters stayed at home.It was an honour to represent Wycombe and I am grateful to everyone I worked with, but I am glad it is over. I will not stand for election again: this is a longstanding, firm and final decision. Why this substack?I am best known for my campaigns: for being outspoken in the cause of liberty under the rule of law and accountable parliamentary democracy as the foundations of our prosperity: things which ought not to be controversial but oddly are in this age of accelerating change and growing polarisation. There is some demand - and considerable opposition! - for me to continue in public life in some way.In this substack, I aim to create and equip a community of like-minded lovers of liberty who appreciate that our problems are not caused by the state being too small and laissez-faire, spending too little with taxes too low, running surpluses, having too little debt or too-tight money over many years: quite the reverse. Society will need a generation of leaders and campaigners equipped to navigate the continuing collapse of the post war social democratic consensus, ultimately funded as it has been by chronically easy money. That is what this substack is for.My recordSteve Baker at…

positions.sound-money:
Every crisis of the last two decades — the financial crash, the cost of living, the collapse of trust in politics — traces back to the same root cause: broken money.

Since 1971, when the last link to gold was severed, governments have funded promises they cannot afford by debasing the currency. Banks create money from nothing every time they make a loan. The Bank of England has admitted as much. Between 1997 and 2010, the UK money supply tripled. During the pandemic, the Bank simply printed money to cover government borrowing. And every time new money enters the system, it benefits those closest to it first — the City, the speculators, the asset-rich — while everyone else watches their savings erode and house prices climb beyond reach.

This is not a technical problem. It is a moral one. Sound money — money whose supply cannot be manipulated at will — is the foundation of a free society. It constrains the state, rewards honest work, and ensures that exchange is founded on value, not illusion.

That is why we advocate for gold and Bitcoin. Not because they are fashionable, but because they are principled. They protect the individual from arbitrary power. And if we are serious about a future that is free, prosperous and just, monetary reform is where it begins.
positions.civil-liberties:
In 1914, a law-abiding Briton could live, work, and travel without the state asking permission or checking their papers. That world is gone.

Today, the British state records your speech as intelligence even when no crime has occurred. It scans your bank accounts without individual suspicion. It can seize money from your account without a court order. It plans to make it illegal to work without a government-issued digital identity. And it is building AI-powered surveillance systems that can track every face, every transaction, and every movement in real time.

Each of these powers was introduced as a targeted, proportionate response to a specific problem: fraud, terrorism, illegal immigration. But the pattern is always the same. What starts as exceptional becomes ordinary. What targets the few expands to monitor the many. The presumption of innocence is quietly replaced by the presumption of suspicion.

The UK has no constitutional guarantee of free speech, no structural limit on surveillance, and no hard legal firewall between the citizen and the state’s expanding digital reach. The infrastructure for total control is being assembled in plain sight — not by tyrants, but by ordinary politicians responding to ordinary incentives.

Fighting for a Free Future exists because these freedoms will not defend themselves. The time to draw hard lines is before they are crossed — not after.
